overview.brief_description: Wedged between the curve of Lake Geneva and the foothills of the Jura, Geneva is Switzerland's most international city — half UN capital, half lakeside resort. The towering Jet d'Eau, the cobbled lanes of the Vieille Ville, and the sweeping promenade of Quai du Mont-Blanc all sit within a fifteen-minute walk of each other, while clear days reveal Mont Blanc itself glittering across the water. A short tram ride away, the headquarters of the UN, WHO, ICRC, and CERN turn the city into a laboratory of global diplomacy. Geneva rewards travelers who balance the postcard set-pieces with the everyday: morning coffee in the Bastions chess garden, fondue in a Carouge bistro, or a swim off the Bains des Pâquis as the sun drops behind the Salève.

sections.title: Lakefront & Mont Blanc Views
sections.content: Geneva's geography is its calling card: the Rhône slices through the centre, Lake Geneva (Lac Léman) opens out to the east, and on bright days Mont Blanc rises 80 km away in perfect frame from the Pont du Mont-Blanc. The 140-metre Jet d'Eau anchors the bay, while wide promenades — Quai du Mont-Blanc on the right bank, Quai Gustave-Ador on the left — link the English Garden, the Brunswick Monument, and the floral clock. The Bains des Pâquis pier doubles as a swimming spot, sauna and lakeside restaurant; in summer locals walk straight off the office and into the water.

sections.title: The International Capital
sections.content: Geneva houses more international organizations per capita than any city on earth. The UN's European headquarters at the Palais des Nations sits in a leafy park populated by peacocks, the ICRC and Red Crescent Museum traces 160 years of humanitarian aid, the WHO, WTO, UNHCR, and ILO all base their global operations here, and CERN's Large Hadron Collider hides under the suburbs out toward France. The result is a city where 40% of residents are foreign nationals, English works as a lingua franca, and you can eat Ethiopian, Lebanese, Sri Lankan, and Sichuanese cooking all within a single Pâquis block.

sections.title: Old Town & Reformation Heritage
sections.content: Climb away from the lake and you enter the Vieille Ville, one of Europe's most intact small Old Towns: cobbled lanes, antique galleries, the Place du Bourg-de-Four where Romans once traded, and Calvin's St. Pierre Cathedral with its 157-step tower view. Geneva was the operational base of the Protestant Reformation, and the Reformation Wall in the Parc des Bastions still stares out at chess players sliding giant pieces across painted squares. Just below, the Maison Tavel — the oldest house in the city — guides you through Geneva's medieval life in painstaking detail.

sections.title: Watchmaking, Chocolate & Quiet Luxury
sections.content: Geneva is the spiritual home of Swiss horology — Patek Philippe, Rolex, Vacheron Constantin, and Piaget all maintain ateliers here, and the Patek Philippe Museum is essential for anyone curious about the craft. The Rue du Rhône delivers the Place Vendôme of luxury shopping, and chocolatiers like Auer, Stettler, Du Rhône, and Favarger turn out pavés glacés and truffles that have set the global standard for over a century. Even the city's rituals lean low-key elegant: a coffee at Café Remor on Place du Cirque, an evening apéro on the Rues Basses, a long Sunday brunch on the lake.

sections.title: Gateway to the Alps & Vineyards
sections.content: Few capitals put you so close to so much. The Salève — Geneva's 1,375-metre home mountain — is fifteen minutes away by tram and cable car. Chamonix and Mont Blanc are an hour by direct bus. Annecy, Lausanne, and the medieval ramparts of Yvoire are all under 75 minutes. North of the city, the terraced Lavaux vineyards (UNESCO World Heritage) drop down toward Montreux; west, the gentle Jura crest opens up panoramic ridge walks. Geneva makes an exceptional Alpine base year-round — ski day-trips in winter, paddleboarding and lake swims in summer, and shoulder-season hikes when the rest of Switzerland is still booked.

sites.name: Jet d'Eau
sites.description: Geneva's iconic fountain shoots a 140-metre column of water into the air at 200 km/h from the Eaux-Vives jetty in Lake Geneva. Originally installed in 1886 as a pressure-release valve for the city's hydraulic power network, it was moved to its current location in 1891 and is now Geneva's signature landmark, visible from across the canton on clear days.
sites.seasonal_notes: Operates roughly March through October daily; runs on a reduced schedule in winter and is shut down during high winds or sub-zero conditions. Illuminated nightly when active. Most photogenic from the Pont du Mont-Blanc and the Bains des Pâquis.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Walk out along the Jetée des Eaux-Vives to feel the spray — bring a light jacket. Best photos from the right bank at sunset with Mont Blanc behind.

sites.name: Old Town (Vieille Ville)
sites.description: Geneva's compact medieval core sits on a low hill on the left bank of the Rhône, centred on the Place du Bourg-de-Four — Roman in origin, now ringed by cafés, fountains and antiquarian galleries. Cobbled lanes lead past Calvin's house, the Hôtel de Ville, ancient guild buildings, and the Maison Tavel before opening onto the Promenade de la Treille and its panoramic bench overlooking the Bastions.
sites.seasonal_notes: Atmospheric year-round; especially photogenic in autumn light and during December's Escalade festival. Steep cobbles can be slippery in rain or snow.
sites.booking_tips: Free walking tours leave from the tourism office on Quai du Mont-Blanc. The 'Geneva Pass' bundles the cathedral tower, Maison Tavel, and the MAH for one price. Combine with lunch on Place du Bourg-de-Four.

sites.name: St. Pierre Cathedral & Archaeological Site
sites.description: Geneva's 12th-century Romanesque-Gothic cathedral served as John Calvin's home pulpit during the Reformation. His wooden chair is still preserved in the nave. The 157-step climb up the North Tower delivers the city's best panoramic view, while the archaeological site beneath the church reveals 2,000 years of layered history from a Roman sanctuary to a 4th-century baptistery.
sites.seasonal_notes: Tower climb closed in heavy weather and during services. The site is significantly less crowded outside the July–August window. Sunday morning services welcome visitors.
sites.booking_tips: Buy a combined ticket (CHF 18) for the tower, archaeological site, and the International Museum of the Reformation next door. Allow 90 minutes for the full circuit.

sites.name: Palais des Nations (UN Geneva)
sites.description: The European headquarters of the United Nations occupies the 1929–1938 Palais des Nations, the largest international conference venue in Europe and the second most-visited UN building after New York. Set in the leafy Ariana Park (home to a small flock of resident peacocks), it hosts the Human Rights Council and disarmament talks year-round. Guided tours take you into the Council Chamber with its Spanish ceiling mural and the Assembly Hall where the League of Nations once sat.
sites.seasonal_notes: Tours run Monday to Friday year-round, with extra weekend slots in July–August. Closed on UN holidays and during high-level summits — check the schedule.
sites.booking_tips: Book online at least 48 hours ahead — passport required for entry security. Allow 90 minutes for the tour plus 30 minutes for the security queue. The Broken Chair monument by Daniel Berset stands on Place des Nations directly outside.

sites.name: International Red Cross & Red Crescent Museum
sites.description: An emotionally weighty museum tracing 160 years of humanitarian action across three permanent themes: Defending Human Dignity, Restoring Family Links, and Reducing Natural Risks. Designed around immersive personal testimonies, archival footage and original objects, it sits directly opposite the UN and is widely considered Geneva's most powerful museum visit.
sites.price_range: CHF 15 (adults), free first Sunday of each month
sites.seasonal_notes: Open Tuesday–Sunday year-round. Particularly resonant on World Humanitarian Day (Aug 19) and Human Rights Day (Dec 10) when special programming runs.
sites.booking_tips: Allow 2 hours — content is dense and the audio guide adds significant context. Combine with a UN tour (across the road) for a half-day International Quarter circuit. Café on the top floor has Mont Blanc views.

sites.name: Patek Philippe Museum
sites.description: Considered the finest watchmaking museum in the world, with two collections: 16th–19th century European horology (enamel work, automata, musical clocks) and the complete history of Patek Philippe from 1839 onward. Each piece is presented with magnifier-level detail and exquisite labelling. A pilgrimage site for anyone interested in watches, miniature painting, or precision engineering.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open Tuesday–Saturday year-round. Closed in late July through mid-August for annual maintenance — verify dates before visiting in summer.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required for individuals; pre-book guided tours (CHF 25, English available) to access workshops. Photography prohibited. Allow 2–3 hours. The on-site bookshop sells out-of-print horology titles.

sites.name: Musée d'Art et d'Histoire (MAH)
sites.description: Switzerland's largest art museum, with seven thousand works on permanent display across archaeology, applied arts and fine art. Highlights include Konrad Witz's 'Miraculous Draught of Fishes' (1444) — the first European painting to depict a real, identifiable landscape (Lake Geneva and the Salève) — alongside Hodler landscapes, Rembrandt portraits, and an exceptional collection of medieval and Byzantine icons.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open Tuesday–Sunday year-round. Late opening Thursdays until 8pm. Temporary exhibitions are usually the strongest draw.
sites.booking_tips: No booking needed for permanent collection. Pick up a free floor plan — the museum is large and easy to get lost in. The café in the courtyard is a good lunch stop.

sites.name: Bains des Pâquis
sites.description: A municipal pier jutting into the bay since 1932, offering free public swimming all summer, a winter sauna and hammam, breakfast, fondue, and a permanently rotating cast of locals from punks to bankers. The lighthouse at the tip is one of Geneva's best free views, and the cooperative-run kiosk serves what most locals consider the city's best café crème.
sites.price_range: Free entry; CHF 2 swim; CHF 22 sauna
sites.seasonal_notes: Swimming season May to September, often through October. Sauna runs October to April with sunrise sessions popular in winter. Open every day of the year except major maintenance shutdowns.
sites.booking_tips: Sauna sessions in winter are mixed-gender and require a swimsuit. Bring your own towel or rent on-site. The fondue evenings (winter) are bookable a week ahead and sell out fast.

sites.name: Parc des Bastions & Reformation Wall
sites.description: Geneva's most loved central park, just outside the Old Town walls. A long arcade of plane trees frames the 100-metre Reformation Wall — a 1909 monument carved with the four giants of the Reformation (Farel, Calvin, Bèze, Knox) and quotes from the movement. Locals come for the giant chess sets played out year-round under the trees, the Café du Bastion, and university students sprawled across the lawns in spring.
sites.seasonal_notes: Year-round, but the chess scene is at its best from April to October. Winter brings a small Christmas market and an ice rink near the Promenade de la Treille bench above.
sites.booking_tips: Bring your own chess pieces if you want to challenge — the giant set is communal and often busy. Combine with the Maison Tavel (5 min walk) and the cathedral (10 min).

sites.name: Carouge
sites.description: Across the Arve from the city centre, Carouge feels less Swiss than Mediterranean — and that's the point. Founded in the 1780s by the Kingdom of Sardinia as a rival to Calvinist Geneva, it was laid out on an Italian grid with low pastel houses, internal courtyards bursting with vines, and a deliberately tolerant atmosphere. Today it is Geneva's craft and food district: independent ceramicists, vintage shops, the city's best Saturday morning market on Place du Marché, and dozens of small bistros and wine bars.
sites.seasonal_notes: Year-round, but the markets and bistro terraces peak from April to October. Christmas in Carouge is its own well-attended event in early December.
sites.booking_tips: Take tram 12 or 18 from Place de Neuve (10 min). For Saturday market mornings arrive by 9am for the best produce. The Place du Marché terrace at Café Gavroche is the classic apéro spot.

sites.name: CERN (Globe of Science & Innovation + Science Gateway)
sites.description: Home of the Large Hadron Collider, CERN opened the spectacular Renzo Piano-designed Science Gateway in 2023, dramatically expanding what visitors can see. Free permanent exhibits walk you through particle physics, the universe at extreme scales, and original equipment from the discovery of the Higgs boson, plus the wooden Globe of Science across the road. Guided tours (free, advance booking) take small groups onto the actual research campus.
sites.seasonal_notes: Science Gateway open Tuesday–Sunday year-round. Free guided campus tours run in English and French — booking opens 14 days in advance and slots disappear within minutes.
sites.booking_tips: Take tram 18 from Cornavin station (25 min) to terminus 'CERN'. Bring photo ID for the campus tour. Allow 3+ hours for both Science Gateway and a tour.

sites.name: Mont Salève (Téléphérique du Salève)
sites.description: Geneva's home mountain rises 1,375 metres directly above the southern edge of the city, technically on the French side of the border. A cable car from Veyrier-Douane (tram 8 from central Geneva, then a 5-minute walk across into France) lifts you to the summit ridge in five minutes, where panoramic terraces look back over the whole Léman basin and out toward Mont Blanc. Excellent paragliding launch, summer hikes, and winter snowshoeing.
sites.best_time: Clear days year-round; sunset is spectacular
sites.seasonal_notes: Cable car runs daily except maintenance closures in November and March (check website). Snowshoeing and Nordic walking in winter; hiking and paragliding April to October.
sites.booking_tips: Bring passport — you're crossing into France. The Tram-Téléphérique combined ticket from Geneva TPG costs CHF 21 and covers the whole journey. Restaurant L'Observatoire on the summit is good for lunch.

sites.name: Geneva Botanical Gardens (Jardin Botanique)
sites.description: Twenty-eight hectares of lakeside greenery just north of the UN, featuring 16,000 plant species, an Alpine rockery, tropical greenhouses, a winter garden and a small zoo of native wildlife (deer, ibex, birds of prey). The Officinal Garden traces the history of medicinal plants used by Geneva's pharmacopoeia, and the Carré Botanique flower bed is replanted seasonally to spectacular effect.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; gardens shine April to September. Greenhouses are particularly welcome in winter for warmth and orchids. Annual plant fair in spring (Vente de Plantes) is a local highlight.
sites.booking_tips: Take bus 1 or 28 to 'Jardin Botanique' or walk 15 minutes north along the lake from Pâquis. Free entry and dog-friendly. Combine with a UN/ICRC half-day.

sites.name: Mouettes Genevoises (Yellow Boats)
sites.description: Geneva's small fleet of bright yellow ferries — the Mouettes — has criss-crossed the bay since 1897. Four short routes connect the two sides of the harbour every 10 minutes and count as ordinary public transport (covered by any TPG ticket). Locals use them to commute; visitors use them as the cheapest possible Lake Geneva cruise.
sites.price_range: CHF 3 (single ticket) or free with a Geneva Transport Card (issued at hotels)
sites.seasonal_notes: Operate daily 7:30am to 9pm in summer; reduced hours in winter (until ~6pm). Run in light snow but cancelled in storms.
sites.booking_tips: Just walk on. The M2 line (Pâquis to Eaux-Vives) gives you the iconic Jet d'Eau crossing. Free yellow boats are the secret of any Geneva itinerary on a budget.

sites.name: Maison Tavel
sites.description: The oldest private house in Geneva (12th century, rebuilt in the 14th) is now the city's history museum. The intimate, low-ceilinged rooms walk you through medieval life with original artefacts, while the standout exhibit is the Magnin Relief — a 30-square-metre 1850s zinc model of Geneva before the city walls were demolished, with every house and shop labelled.
sites.best_time: Wet afternoons or after the cathedral climb
sites.seasonal_notes: Open Tuesday–Sunday year-round. Free permanent collection makes it an excellent rainy-day option. Particularly popular during the Escalade festival weekend in December.
sites.booking_tips: Allow 1 hour. Combine with the cathedral and Place du Bourg-de-Four (3-minute walk). Free guided tours in French on the first Sunday of the month.

sites.name: Lavaux Vineyards (Day Trip)
sites.description: A UNESCO World Heritage cascade of terraced vineyards plunging down to Lake Geneva between Lausanne and Montreux — 30 km of dry-stone walls built up over eight centuries by Cistercian monks. Trains stop at tiny stations like Epesses and Cully, from which you can walk the Chemin du Lavaux ridge among Chasselas vines with the Alps reflected in the water below. Geneva to Lavaux is 50 minutes by direct train.
sites.price_range: CHF 30 round-trip train; wine tastings CHF 15–30
sites.seasonal_notes: Walking is best from April to October. The September Caves Ouvertes weekend opens private cellars to the public — book accommodations months ahead.
sites.booking_tips: Buy a Léman Pass for unlimited regional trains and lake boats. Hike from Lutry to Saint-Saphorin (14 km, mostly downhill). The Lavaux Vinorama in Rivaz introduces 70+ local producers under one roof.

neighborhoods.name: Vieille Ville (Old Town)
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's medieval and Renaissance core, perched on a low hill above the left bank of the Rhône. Cobbled lanes, antiquarian galleries, ancient guild buildings, the cathedral and the seat of the Republic of Geneva all coexist in a compact half-square-kilometre. Quiet by Swiss standards even in peak season — the city's quieter, more cerebral counterpoint to the lake.

location.description: On the hill immediately south of the Rhône, bordered by Plainpalais to the south and the Rues Basses shopping district to the north.
borders: Place de Neuve and Parc des Bastions (west)

history.overview: Continuously inhabited since the Romans founded Genava on this hill above the Rhône. Became a fortified episcopal seat in the Middle Ages and the operational base of the Protestant Reformation under Calvin in the 16th century. The city walls were demolished in 1850, but the medieval street plan and most of the buildings survive intact.
history.significance: Birthplace of the Protestant Reformation, capital of independent Geneva for centuries, and the city's symbolic heart. Calvin preached at St. Pierre; the Declaration of the Rights of Man was drafted in the Hôtel de Ville's Salle de l'Alabama, which also hosted the founding of the Red Cross in 1864.
notable_events: Roman foundation of Genava (1st century BC)
Reformation declared in Geneva (1536)
Founding of the International Committee of the Red Cross at the Hôtel de Ville (1864)
Demolition of the city walls (1850)

transit: Largely pedestrianised — buses 36 and 3 skirt the edges
10-minute walk from Cornavin main station via Pont du Mont-Blanc
Closest tram stop: Place de Neuve (lines 12, 15, 18)
practical_info.safety: Among the safest neighbourhoods in Europe — well-patrolled, lit, and busy until late.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Mid-morning for the antique shops and cafés; golden hour for the cathedral tower view; December for the Escalade festival re-enactments.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent but steep — cobbled climbs from the Rhône to the cathedral plateau require comfortable shoes.

known_for: Longest wooden bench in the world (Promenade de la Treille)
best_for: Anyone wanting calm in a global city

attractions.name: St. Pierre Cathedral & Archaeological Site
attractions.description: Calvin's home cathedral, with a 157-step tower climb and an underground site exposing 2,000 years of layered history.
attractions.appeal: City's best panoramic view and the most important Reformation site in the world.

attractions.name: Place du Bourg-de-Four
attractions.description: Geneva's oldest square, used as a Roman trading post and now the social heart of the Old Town with cafés, fountains and antique shops.
attractions.appeal: Pure Old Town atmosphere without the museum entry fees.

attractions.name: Maison Tavel
attractions.description: Oldest house in Geneva, now the city history museum — home to the remarkable Magnin Relief of 1850s Geneva.
attractions.appeal: Free, intimate, and surprisingly moving social history.

dining.name: Café Papon
dining.known_for: Classic fondue, filets de perche and a sun-trap terrace below the cathedral wall

dining.name: Les Armures
dining.known_for: Geneva's most famous fondue institution, frequented by every visiting head of state for decades
dining.atmosphere: Heavy wood beams, candlelight, formal but warm.

activities.name: Reformation Walk
activities.description: Loop from the Reformation Wall to St. Pierre via Calvin's house and the Auditoire de Calvin.

activities.name: Cathedral Tower Climb
activities.description: 157 steps for the best panoramic view in Geneva — lake, Mont Blanc, the Jura and the Old Town rooftops.

stay_here_if: You want quiet, atmospheric streets within walking distance of everything
You're interested in Reformation history or Swiss culture
You prefer boutique hotels in historic buildings
You're happy to climb cobbled hills
avoid_if: You want a buzzy nightlife scene on your doorstep
You're travelling with mobility constraints (steep, uneven streets)
You're after a lakeside view from your window
insider_tips: The Promenade de la Treille is home to the longest wooden bench in the world (120 m)
Carved on the Place du Bourg-de-Four fountain are the dates of every major political event in Genevan history — worth a slow read
Most antique shops close on Mondays — plan around it

neighborhoods.name: Pâquis
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's most cosmopolitan, multi-ethnic and around-the-clock neighbourhood — three blocks deep along the right-bank lakefront. Migration over four generations has made it the city's culinary United Nations: Lebanese bakeries next to Sri Lankan canteens next to Sichuan noodle bars. By night it doubles as Geneva's red-light district, which keeps prices honest and the streets lively.

location.description: Right bank of the Rhône, immediately behind the Quai du Mont-Blanc and the main train station Cornavin.
landmarks: Mausoleum of the Duke of Brunswick

history.overview: Originally fishing hamlets and pastures outside the city walls, absorbed into Geneva in 1850. The 20th century turned it into the city's main hospitality and migrant district, anchored by the railway station and the grand lakefront hotels.
history.significance: Where most visitors first stay, and where Geneva's working-class and international identities have always met.
notable_events: Construction of Cornavin station (1858)
Inauguration of the Bains des Pâquis (1932)
Wave of post-war migration that built today's culinary scene (1960s–1990s)

transit: Cornavin train station is at the top of the neighbourhood
Mouettes (yellow boats) from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives every 10 minutes
practical_info.safety: Generally safe and busy. The Rue de Berne / Rue de Monthoux strip is the city's red-light area — well-lit and policed but not for everyone after midnight.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Late morning for fresh-baked manakish and coffee; sunset on the Bains des Pâquis pier; weekend brunch.
practical_info.walkability: Flat, dense, walkable — ten minutes from end to end.

known_for: Lakefront promenade and Bains des Pâquis
Lebanese, Sri Lankan and Eritrean restaurants
Affordable hotels close to the station
Best winter sauna in the city

attractions.name: Bains des Pâquis
attractions.description: Free public swim spot in summer, sauna and hammam in winter, with the city's best café crème all year round.
attractions.appeal: The most Genevan of Genevan rituals.

attractions.name: Brunswick Monument
attractions.description: Ornate Gothic Revival mausoleum on the lakefront, built to honour the Duke of Brunswick whose estate financed it.
attractions.appeal: Strangely Venetian on the Geneva shore — a great photo subject.

dining.name: Le Comptoir des Pâquis
dining.known_for: Honest mezze, natural wines and the city's friendliest service

dining.name: Le Relais de l'Entrecôte
dining.known_for: Single-dish menu of green-sauced entrecôte and frites — Paris exported intact
dining.atmosphere: Bistro classic, queues out the door at 7pm.

activities.name: Sunset on the Pier
activities.description: Coffee or apéro at the Bains kiosk as the Jet d'Eau lights up.

activities.name: Mouettes Crossing
activities.description: Take the yellow boat from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives and back — cheapest lake cruise in Europe.

stay_here_if: You're arriving by train and want a five-minute walk to your hotel
You want a diverse food scene at every price point
You want lake access without paying lakefront prices
You'll be out late
avoid_if: You're sensitive to street noise and tram bells
You're travelling with very young children who'll find the red-light strip confronting
You want a quiet, leafy retreat
insider_tips: The Bains des Pâquis sauna is mixed-gender — bring a swimsuit
The hidden Sri Lankan restaurants on Rue de Neuchâtel are cheaper and better than the tourist-facing places on Rue de Berne
Notre-Dame Basilica's evening choral concerts (Saturdays) are free

neighborhoods.name: Eaux-Vives
neighborhoods.character: Eaux-Vives is the lakeside left bank: leafy, residential, slightly more refined than Pâquis across the water. Quai Gustave-Ador and the English Garden anchor the lakefront, while inland the neighbourhood spreads into elegant 19th-century apartment blocks, embassies, and a tight grid of independent cafés and bistros around Rue de Montchoisy.

location.description: Left bank of the Rhône, immediately east of the Old Town and stretching along the lake.
landmarks: Jet d'Eau and Jetée des Eaux-Vives

history.overview: Vineyards and country houses until incorporated into Geneva in 1798, urbanised through the 19th century with the elegant Quai Gustave-Ador and the English Garden as showpiece projects. The new Léman Express train link (2019) put the neighbourhood on the regional rail map and triggered a wave of high-end residential development.
history.significance: The civic showcase of the lakefront, where every postcard photo of Geneva is actually taken from.
notable_events: Annexation into Geneva (1798)
Construction of the Quai Gustave-Ador (1851)
Installation of the Jet d'Eau (1891)
Opening of Léman Express at Eaux-Vives station (2019)

transit: Eaux-Vives station on Léman Express (3 min to Cornavin, 38 min to Annemasse)
practical_info.safety: Very safe at all hours, even in the parks.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Late afternoon for golden light on the Jet d'Eau; spring for the Jardin Anglais; summer evening picnics in Parc La Grange.
practical_info.walkability: Very walkable, flat along the lake. The southern slopes toward Champel are mildly hilly.

known_for: Quai Gustave-Ador and the Jet d'Eau
English Garden and the Floral Clock
Parc La Grange's rose garden and summer concerts
Embassy quarter and high-end residential blocks
New Eaux-Vives station and surrounding gastronomy
best_for: Visitors with kids (parks and the lake)
Anyone wanting that Mont Blanc photo

attractions.name: Jet d'Eau Jetty
attractions.description: Walk the pier to feel the spray of Geneva's signature water column.
attractions.appeal: Unrivalled photo op of Geneva's most famous landmark.

attractions.name: Jardin Anglais
attractions.description: Lakefront park with the famous Floral Clock and a beautiful jet-spray fountain.
attractions.appeal: Free, central, scenic — Geneva 101 in 30 minutes.

attractions.name: Parc La Grange
attractions.description: Geneva's largest rose garden (12,000 plants) and the venue for the free Musiques en été summer concerts.
attractions.appeal: Local park life at its best.

dining.name: Café du Soleil
dining.known_for: Frequently listed among Geneva's top fondue spots (1834 institution)

dining.name: Tsé Fung
dining.known_for: Michelin-starred dim sum and Peking duck inside the La Réserve hotel

activities.name: Lakefront Run
activities.description: A flat, scenic 8 km loop from the English Garden out to Genève-Plage and back.

activities.name: Floral Clock Selfie
activities.description: Geneva's most photographed flowerbed, replanted seasonally.

stay_here_if: You want a lakeside neighbourhood without the buzz of Pâquis
You're travelling with kids and want parks and the lake
You prefer residential charm over central commotion
You're using the Léman Express for day trips to France
avoid_if: You're on a tight budget — accommodation is pricier here
You need to be next to the Old Town museums
insider_tips: Musiques en été (free summer concerts in Parc La Grange) runs all of July and August
The Eaux-Vives waterfront has Geneva's only true sandy public beach (free) — Genève-Plage to the east is the pay version with pools and a high-dive
From Quai Gustave-Ador you get the best photo of the Old Town across the harbour

neighborhoods.name: Carouge
neighborhoods.character: An entirely separate municipality across the Arve from Geneva proper, founded by Italians in the 1780s and built on a generous Mediterranean grid. Pastel houses, internal courtyards, communal vines, and a Saturday market on Place du Marché — Carouge feels half a continent away from buttoned-up central Geneva. It's the city's craft, design and food district.

location.description: Two kilometres south-west of central Geneva across the Arve river.

history.overview: Planned and laid out in the 1780s by Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia as a Catholic, free-trade rival to Calvinist Geneva. The Sardinian engineers used Turin's grid as a template, with single-storey houses, communal vine pergolas in the courtyards, and a deliberate religious tolerance that drew artisans from across Catholic Europe.
history.significance: Switzerland's most successful piece of 18th-century urban planning and Geneva's enduring counterweight to Protestant rigidity. Joined Geneva canton in 1816 but kept its own commune.
notable_events: Foundation by Charter of Victor Amadeus III (1786)
Joining of the Canton of Geneva (1816)
Designation as a federally protected historic town (1995)

transit: Trams 12 and 18 from Place de Neuve (10 min)
practical_info.safety: Among the safest neighbourhoods in the canton.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Saturday morning for the market; Thursday evening when restaurants stay open later; December for Christmas in Carouge.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent — flat, grid-planned, mostly pedestrian streets.

known_for: Saturday market on Place du Marché

attractions.name: Place du Marché
attractions.description: The communal heart of Carouge, hosting markets Wednesdays and Saturdays, plus open-air concerts in summer.

attractions.name: Église Sainte-Croix
attractions.description: Neoclassical 1781 parish church reflecting Carouge's Catholic foundation, set on the central square.
attractions.appeal: Architecture and quiet, with regular free organ recitals.

dining.name: Café des Négociants
dining.known_for: Classic Lyonnaise bouchon-style cooking — onglet, andouillette, île flottante

dining.name: Olivier de Provence
dining.known_for: Truffle ravioli and lavender crème brûlée since 1969

activities.name: Saturday Market Browse
activities.description: Fruit, cheese, charcuterie and flowers — the city's best market morning.

activities.name: Artisan Studio Crawl
activities.description: Loop through the boutiques on Rue Saint-Joseph and Rue Saint-Victor for ceramics, goldsmiths and bookbinders.

stay_here_if: You want an authentic, lived-in feel away from tourist Geneva
You love food markets and bistros
You're staying multiple nights and want a residential base
You're travelling with a partner on a slow itinerary
avoid_if: You want to wake up and walk straight to the cathedral
You're only in town for a single night between flights
You need to be next to the lake
insider_tips: Many Carouge courtyards are open — push the heavy wooden doors to discover communal vine pergolas and gardens
Thursday is Carouge's quiet long-lunch day; many shops shut for two hours after 12:30
Christmas in Carouge (first weekend of December) is a charming local event — book a table well in advance

neighborhoods.name: Plainpalais & Quartier des Bains
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's student, market and contemporary-art neighbourhood, organised around the vast triangular Plaine de Plainpalais — once a parade ground, now a flea market site, festival venue, and the city's largest open public space. The university spills out at one end; the gallery-heavy Quartier des Bains anchors the other.

location.description: South-west of the Old Town, across the Boulevard du Théâtre.
landmarks: MAMCO (Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art)

history.overview: Originally a flood plain of the Arve used as a parade and execution ground until the 19th century, when the university and surrounding boulevards were built. The Quartier des Bains takes its name from the public baths once located there, and from the 1990s became Geneva's contemporary-art gallery quarter.
history.significance: Geneva's intellectual and creative engine — student life, contemporary art, public protest, and the city's biggest flea market all in the same square kilometre.
notable_events: University of Geneva founded by Calvin (1559); current Uni Mail campus opened 1992
Opening of MAMCO (1994)
Launch of the Nuit des Bains gallery night (2002)

transit: Tram 14 along Boulevard de la Cluse
Walkable from the Old Town in 10 minutes
practical_info.safety: Very safe, busy with students and joggers at most hours.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Wednesday and Saturday mornings for the food market; Sunday for the flea market; Thursday evenings during the Nuit des Bains gallery openings.
practical_info.walkability: Flat, broad pavements, excellent for walking and cycling.

known_for: Wednesday/Saturday food market and Sunday flea market
Cinema Spoutnik and the art-house circuit
best_for: Anyone who finds the Old Town too polished

attractions.name: MAMCO
attractions.description: Switzerland's largest museum of contemporary art, in a former factory at the heart of the Quartier des Bains.
attractions.appeal: Bold programming and free entry on the first Sunday of the month.

attractions.name: Patek Philippe Museum
attractions.description: Two storeys of horological history — see attractions entry for detail.

attractions.name: Plaine de Plainpalais Flea Market
attractions.description: Sunday flea market sprawling across the plain; among the largest in Switzerland.

dining.name: Brasserie des Halles de l'Île
dining.known_for: Set lunch menus, river terrace, gallery exhibitions inside

dining.name: Café du Cirque
dining.known_for: Filets de perche done well at a fair price
dining.atmosphere: Tiled and corner-pub, a local fixture.

activities.name: Nuit des Bains
activities.description: Three nights a year (spring/autumn) when 20+ galleries throw simultaneous openings.

activities.name: Plainpalais Sunday Flea Market
activities.description: Loop the entire plaine for second-hand books, vinyl, vintage clothing and oddities.

stay_here_if: You want a creative, less touristy base
You're interested in contemporary art
You like markets, cinema and student energy
You want lower hotel prices closer to the centre
avoid_if: You want lake views from your hotel window
You're staying only one night and want the Old Town on your doorstep
insider_tips: Wednesday is the second farmers' market day on Plainpalais — quieter and easier than Saturday
Nuit des Bains dates are published in early spring; if you can time a trip, do it
The University's Uni Mail building has a free public rooftop with one of the best southern views in town

neighborhoods.name: International Quarter (Nations)
neighborhoods.character: Leafy, calm, half-park, half-glass-and-concrete diplomatic complex around the Palais des Nations. The flags of every UN member state line Place des Nations, Daniel Berset's 12-metre Broken Chair stands as a monument to landmine victims, and within walking distance are the WHO, ICRC, WIPO, WTO and the Botanical Garden. Not a place to stay — but an essential half-day visit.

location.description: Two kilometres north of Cornavin along Rue de Lausanne; bordered by the lake to the east and the Geneva-Lausanne road to the west.
landmarks: International Red Cross & Red Crescent Museum

history.overview: Until the 1920s this was farmland and country estates. Selected as the seat of the League of Nations in 1920 and remained a global diplomatic capital after the UN moved into the building in 1946. Continuous expansion through the 1960s–2010s as new agencies arrived.
history.significance: Home of the United Nations Office at Geneva (UNOG), the second-largest UN duty station, hosting most disarmament and human-rights negotiations.
notable_events: Inauguration of the Palais des Nations (1936)
Reopening as the UN European Headquarters (1946)
Inauguration of the Broken Chair (1997)
Opening of the WHO Member-State Lounge (2025 expansion)

transit: Tram 15 from Cornavin to Place des Nations (10 min)
Bicycle path from the lakefront in 20 minutes
practical_info.safety: Calm and very safe. Some streets close during major UN sessions and require ID for access.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Weekday mornings to combine a UN tour with the ICRC Museum and Ariana Park.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent if linear — most sights cluster around Place des Nations.

attractions.name: Palais des Nations
attractions.description: Guided tours of the UN's European headquarters.
attractions.appeal: One of the most distinctive 20th-century institutional buildings in the world.

attractions.name: Broken Chair
attractions.description: Daniel Berset's 12-metre wooden chair with one shattered leg — a campaign against landmines.

dining.name: Restaurant Vieux-Bois
dining.known_for: The Geneva hotel school's public restaurant — excellent value classic French

dining.name: La Perle du Lac
dining.known_for: Lakeside terrace, regional produce, Mont Blanc views

activities.name: Diplomatic Walking Loop
activities.description: UN → ICRC Museum → Ariana Park → Botanical Garden → lakefront (3 hours).

activities.name: Free Botanical Garden
activities.description: Twenty-eight hectares of formal gardens and tropical greenhouses with a small wildlife collection.

stay_here_if: You're here for a UN meeting or conference
You want a calmer, greener base than central Geneva
You prefer corporate hotels with conference facilities
avoid_if: You want walking access to the Old Town and nightlife
You're on a short trip and don't want to spend evenings commuting
insider_tips: UN tour tickets require a passport and at least 48 hours advance booking
Ariana Park is free and home to peacocks who wander between sessions of the General Assembly
The ICRC Museum's audio guide is essential — without it you'll miss most of the depth

spring: Walk the lakefront from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives as the chestnuts come into leaf
Catch the Geneva International Motor Show or the Salon du Livre (April)
Browse the year's Saturday markets restarting in Carouge and Plainpalais
Hike the Salève ridge after the snow melts (April–May)
Watch the Bains des Pâquis pier reopen for swimming (May)
summer: Swim at Bains des Pâquis or Genève-Plage
Picnic in Parc La Grange during the free Musiques en été concerts (July–August)
Cycle the Lavaux vineyard route between Lausanne and Montreux
Watch Fête de Genève fireworks over the lake (early August)
Take the cable car up the Salève for sunset over the Alps
autumn: Drink new wine at the Caves Ouvertes weekends across the Geneva countryside (September)
Hike the Jura crest for sweeping autumn colour
Visit MAMCO and the Quartier des Bains galleries during Nuit des Bains (October)
Try seasonal game menus (chasse) in Old Town bistros
Watch the Course de l'Escalade running race weave through the Old Town (late November)
winter: Use the Bains des Pâquis sauna at sunrise
Day-trip to ski at Les Houches, Flaine or La Clusaz (1 hour by bus)
Eat fondue or raclette in the Old Town
Walk the Christmas markets in the Jardin Anglais and Carouge (December)
Watch the Escalade festival re-enactments and chocolate-pot smashing (December 11–12)

fine_dining.name: Le Chat-Botté
signature_dishes: Léman féra carpaccio with caviar and lemon confit
fine_dining.atmosphere: Refined lakefront dining room inside the Beau-Rivage hotel, with Mont Blanc views and white-glove service
fine_dining.best_time: Dinner, especially for the seasonal tasting menu
fine_dining.booking_tips: Reserve 2-3 weeks ahead; request a window table for the Mont Blanc view; jacket required for men at dinner
fine_dining.local_tips: Lunch service offers a more affordable entry point to the cooking. Pair with a Lavaux Chasselas from the deep cellar.

fine_dining.name: Bayview by Michel Roth
signature_dishes: Tarte au chocolat noir grand cru
fine_dining.atmosphere: Sleek glass-walled dining room on top of the Hotel President with the city's most expansive lake panorama
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book the chef's tasting menu for the kitchen view; request bay-facing window
fine_dining.local_tips: Roth's wine pairings lean heavily on Geneva-region producers, including small AOC Chasselas growers worth exploring.

fine_dining.name: Tsé Fung
signature_dishes: Peking duck served in two services
Steamed dim sum trolley at lunch
fine_dining.atmosphere: Dramatic Chinese-modern room inside La Réserve hotel with red lacquer accents and lake-garden access
fine_dining.best_time: Lunch dim sum or weekend Peking duck dinner
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book Peking duck 24 hours ahead; dim sum lunch is the best-value Michelin meal in the city
fine_dining.location: La Réserve, Route de Lausanne 301 (15 min from centre)
fine_dining.local_tips: The only Cantonese restaurant in Switzerland with a Michelin star — worth the cab ride.

fine_dining.name: Domaine de Châteauvieux
signature_dishes: Whole roast Bresse chicken for two
fine_dining.atmosphere: 16th-century farmhouse in vineyards 20 minutes from the city, with terrace overlooking the Rhône valley
fine_dining.best_time: Long weekend lunch in spring or autumn
fine_dining.booking_tips: Book the tasting menu and request an outdoor table; reserve at least a month ahead in autumn (game season)
fine_dining.local_tips: Geneva's most celebrated rural-fine-dining experience; Philippe Chevrier has held two stars for over two decades.

casual_dining.name: Café du Soleil
signature_dishes: Filets de perche meunière with frites
casual_dining.atmosphere: 1834 institution in Petit-Saconnex — beam ceilings, white tablecloths, packed local crowd
casual_dining.booking_tips: Reserve a table by the window or in the back room; can be booked online via the website
casual_dining.local_tips: Genevans will argue endlessly about the city's best fondue, but Café du Soleil is on every shortlist for a reason.

casual_dining.name: Les Armures
signature_dishes: Roesti with cheese and fried egg
casual_dining.atmosphere: Old Town stalwart with armoured-knight decor and heavy wood beams; every visiting head of state since the 1980s has eaten here
casual_dining.booking_tips: Reserve a back-room booth for atmosphere; closed Sundays in low season
casual_dining.local_tips: Touristy but consistently excellent fondue. Ask for the kirsch-flambé version if you want the full ceremony.

casual_dining.name: Café du Centre
signature_dishes: Filets de perche from Lake Geneva
casual_dining.atmosphere: Belle Époque brasserie with a vast central island bar, dating to 1871 — the city's oldest continuous brasserie
casual_dining.best_time: Long lunch on Place du Molard
casual_dining.booking_tips: Walk-in friendly at lunch; reserve dinner Thursday–Saturday
casual_dining.local_tips: The terrace on Place du Molard is one of the best people-watching spots in central Geneva.

casual_dining.name: Le Relais de l'Entrecôte
signature_dishes: Sliced entrecôte with green herb sauce and frites — single dish, two services
casual_dining.atmosphere: Classic Parisian-style bistro with no menu choice and queues out the door
casual_dining.best_time: Early dinner (6:30pm) to avoid the worst queues
casual_dining.booking_tips: Walk-ins only; queues from 7:30pm onwards; weekday lunch is fastest
casual_dining.local_tips: Same green-sauce formula as the Paris original. Save room for the profiteroles.

casual_dining.name: Café des Négociants
casual_dining.atmosphere: Buzzy bistro in Carouge with a vine-shaded terrace; the strongest natural-wine list in town
casual_dining.booking_tips: Book a terrace table for warm-weather lunches; closed Sundays
casual_dining.local_tips: Order the dish of the day chalked above the bar — usually offal or seasonal game from Geneva-region farms.

street_food.name: Manakish & shawarma on Rue de Berne (Pâquis)
street_food.atmosphere: Stand-up counters and small storefronts, open into the small hours
street_food.booking_tips: No bookings — just walk in. Parfums de Beyrouth and Boulangerie du Levant are reliably excellent
street_food.location: Rue de Berne and Rue de Neuchâtel, Pâquis
street_food.local_tips: Geneva's cheapest sit-down dinner is a manakish and labneh combo for under CHF 12.

street_food.name: Plainpalais Wednesday Food Trucks
street_food.atmosphere: Open-air park-side market with 15+ rotating trucks
street_food.local_tips: Cash is still useful at some trucks. Many do happy-hour specials Friday evenings.

coffee_shops.name: Boréal Coffee Shop
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Minimalist Scandinavian-style cafés, the closest thing Geneva has to a Melbourne or Oslo coffee scene
coffee_shops.location: Multiple branches: Old Town (Rue Verdaine), Pâquis, Eaux-Vives, Cornavin
coffee_shops.local_tips: The Rue Verdaine flagship has the most extensive single-origin offering.

coffee_shops.name: Bains des Pâquis Kiosk
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Communal pier-side counter open every day of the year — locals' default morning stop
coffee_shops.location: Bains des Pâquis pier, Quai du Mont-Blanc
coffee_shops.local_tips: Run by a cooperative — profits fund the pier and sauna. Order at the counter, take outside.

coffee_shops.name: Café Remor
coffee_shops.atmosphere: Belle Époque corner café with a marble bar and the city's longest-running pâtisserie counter
coffee_shops.local_tips: Their truffes au chocolat and merveilles (donut puffs) are the platonic ideal of a Genevan tea-time.

bars.name: Atelier Cocktail Club
signature_drinks: Negroni variations on a barrel-aged tap
bars.atmosphere: Dimly lit, jazz-soundtracked back room in the Old Town with a serious bar program
bars.local_tips: Sit at the bar to watch the team work. Frequently ranked in Switzerland's top 5 cocktail bars.

bars.name: Café des Banques
signature_drinks: Local Chasselas wines by the glass
bars.atmosphere: Standing-room corner spot in the banking district that fills with Geneva's finance crowd from 6pm
bars.local_tips: Free pretzels and saucisson with apéro orders — Geneva tradition.

bars.name: Yvette de Marseille
bars.atmosphere: Eccentric Marseille-tinged dive with mismatched chairs and Côte d'Azur kitsch; beloved late-night spot
bars.local_tips: Open until 2am most nights. Ask the bartender for an unlisted pastis flight.

food_tours.name: Geneva Food & Walking Tour
highlights: Chocolatier stop at Auer or Stettler
Old Town wine bar visit with Chasselas pairing
Stop at a charcuterie for longeole sausage
food_tours.location: Meets in front of the cathedral
food_tours.local_tips: Run by Edible Geneva and Geneva Tour — both excellent. Book a week ahead, smaller groups (max 8).

food_tours.name: Carouge Market & Bistro Tour
highlights: Producer introductions at Place du Marché
Cheese tasting with an AOC fromager
food_tours.local_tips: Better tour than the central Geneva equivalents — smaller, friendlier, more local.

cooking_classes.name: École Hôtelière de Genève — Public Cooking Workshops
cooking_classes.location: Avenue de la Paix 12, near the UN
cooking_classes.local_tips: Genuinely high-level instruction — most participants are aspiring chefs and serious home cooks.

cooking_classes.name: Stettler Chocolate Workshop
cooking_classes.local_tips: Book the December classes early — Stettler's chocolate sells out before Christmas.

markets.name: Plainpalais Market (Marché de Plainpalais)
markets.type: Twice-weekly farmers' market + Sunday flea market
highlights: Local cheeses including Gruyère AOP and Tomme Vaudoise
Sunday flea market (separate stalls, vintage and antiques)
markets.best_time: Saturday morning for the freshest selection
markets.local_tips: Brunch nearby at Brasserie des Halles de l'Île after the market round.

markets.name: Carouge Saturday Market
highlights: Bistros open early for market-day coffee
markets.local_tips: Pair with a long lunch at a Carouge bistro — Café des Négociants or Olivier de Provence.

markets.name: Marché Pâquis (Place de la Navigation)
highlights: Daily fruit and veg, Tuesday to Saturday
Multicultural stalls reflecting the Pâquis demographic
markets.best_time: Morning for produce, late afternoon for prepared foods
markets.local_tips: Geneva's most multicultural market — Lebanese, Sri Lankan and Iberian producers all represented.

spring: Wild garlic (ail des ours) used in soups, butters and pestos
Fresh féra and omble chevalier from Lake Geneva (May reopening of fishing season)
Easter lamb in Old Town bistros
Cardons genevois — Geneva's protected thistle vegetable, in spring re-emergence
summer: Filets de perche from Lake Geneva, served lakeside
Geneva-region Chasselas wines from the new vintage
Fresh strawberries and stone fruit from Satigny and Russin
Cold soups and salads on outdoor terraces
Pavé glacé chocolates eaten alongside lake swims
autumn: Chasse (game) season — wild boar, venison, hare in Old Town bistros
Cardons genevois with gratin sauce, served at Christmas markets in their full season
Wild mushrooms (cèpes, chanterelles) from the Jura
New wine releases at Caves Ouvertes weekends
Pumpkin and chestnut dishes in casual bistros
winter: Raclette with potatoes and pickled onions
Vacherin Mont d'Or — the runny seasonal AOC cheese
Marmite de l'Escalade — chocolate cauldrons filled with marzipan vegetables (December)
Hot vin chaud (mulled wine) at the Old Town Christmas market

destinations.whyGo: The Olympic capital and Vaud's lively university city — terraced lakefront, the Olympic Museum, a vibrant Flon nightlife district and the most spectacular metro climb in Europe (M2). Excellent springboard to the Lavaux vineyards.
highlights: M2 metro descent from the cathedral to Ouchy
Springboard to the Lavaux UNESCO vineyards

destinations.whyGo: Riviera-feel resort at the head of Lake Geneva, home to the Montreux Jazz Festival, the Chillon Castle peninsula and the lakeside Freddie Mercury statue. Pairs perfectly with a stop in the Lavaux vineyards.
highlights: Lavaux vineyards on the way back

destinations.whyGo: Storybook canal town nicknamed 'the Venice of the Alps,' with a turquoise lake, pastel canalside houses and the medieval Palais de l'Île. One of the most scenic day trips in France.
highlights: Climbing the château for old town views

destinations.whyGo: France's flagship Alpine resort directly beneath Mont Blanc. The Aiguille du Midi cable car takes you to 3,842 m for one of Europe's most thrilling viewpoints. Year-round destination — skiing in winter, hiking and climbing in summer.

directBus.frequency: Several daily via FlixBus, Mountain Drop-offs

highlights: Mer de Glace glacier and train
Skiing in winter (Les Houches, Brevent, Grands Montets)
Hiking the Tour du Mont Blanc in summer

destinations.whyGo: A perfectly preserved medieval village on the French shore of Lake Geneva, classified one of the 'most beautiful villages of France.' Flower-draped stone houses, the Jardin des Cinq Sens, and the easiest possible international boat-trip from Geneva.
highlights: Combine with Nyon on the same boat-loop

destinations.whyGo: Switzerland's largest city and financial capital — Kunsthaus art, the Bahnhofstrasse luxury shopping mile, riverside swimming, and an energetic post-industrial nightlife scene in Zürich West.

Switzerland's capital and a UNESCO-listed medieval Old Town wrapped in a loop of the river Aare. Famous for the Zytglogge clock tower, six kilometres of arcaded shopping streets, the Bear Park, and (in summer) the local ritual of floating down the Aare.

France's gastronomic capital and a UNESCO World Heritage city, with the Renaissance traboules of Vieux Lyon, the Roman amphitheatres of Fourvière, and the bouchon bistro tradition that defines French comfort cooking.
highlights: Basilica of Fourvière and panoramic terrace
Les Halles de Lyon Paul Bocuse food hall

destinations.whyGo: Italian-speaking Ticino — palm trees, lakes, and three UNESCO-listed medieval castles in Bellinzona. A taste of Italy without leaving Switzerland.

directTrain.journeyTime: Approx. 4h15 (via Zurich or via the Gotthard base tunnel)

destinations.whyGo: The launchpad for the Jungfrau region — Jungfraujoch (the 'Top of Europe' at 3,454 m), Lauterbrunnen's valley of 72 waterfalls, and the Schilthorn revolving restaurant. Switzerland's signature mountain experience.

directTrain.journeyTime: Approx. 2h45 to Interlaken Ost (one change at Bern)

destinations.whyGo: Belle Époque French spa town on the south shore of Lake Geneva, famous for its bottled mineral water, casino, lakeside palace hotels and a thermal spa complex. The easiest international ferry day-trip from Geneva.

directBus.stationInGeneva: Geneva Mont-Blanc Pier (CGN boats) or via Lausanne ferry
directBus.journeyTime: Approx. 2h35 by lake (or via Lausanne short ferry from Ouchy)

highlights: Funicular up to Évian's upper town
Combine with Lausanne via the cross-lake ferry

destinations.whyGo: 30 km of UNESCO-listed terraced vineyards plunging down to Lake Geneva, built over eight centuries. Walking the Chemin du Lavaux is among the most spectacular short hikes in Europe.

directTrain.journeyTime: Approx. 50–55 minutes to Lutry, Cully or Epesses

highlights: Lavaux Vinorama wine centre in Rivaz

destinations.whyGo: Hilltop walled village in Fribourg canton, home to the Gruyère cheese tradition and the offbeat HR Giger Alien-art museum. Compact, photogenic and perfect for a half-day.
highlights: Cailler chocolate factory in nearby Broc

activities.name: Lavaux Vineyard Walk
activities.description: Hike the UNESCO-listed Chemin du Lavaux ridge as the Chasselas vines come into leaf and the lake mirrors the snow-capped Alps. Stop at family cellars along the way for tastings of the new vintage.
activities.seasonal_notes: Best walked Lutry → Saint-Saphorin (14 km, mostly downhill). Combine with a CGN paddle-steamer back to Geneva for a perfect circular day.
activities.booking_tips: Buy a regional Léman Pass for unlimited train and lake travel. Caves Ouvertes Vaudoises typically falls on the last weekend of May.

activities.name: Caves Ouvertes Genevoises
activities.description: Last weekend of May: dozens of Geneva-canton winemakers open cellar doors for free tastings. Free buses link the major estates of Satigny, Russin, Dardagny and Bernex.
activities.best_time: Last weekend of May, all day
activities.seasonal_notes: Easily the most loved spring weekend in Geneva — book a hotel weeks ahead. The wine route through the western countryside is one of the most beautiful corners of the canton.
activities.booking_tips: Pick up the official tasting passport at the tourist office. Start in Satigny (15 min from Cornavin) and work west toward Russin.

activities.name: Mont Salève Hike
activities.description: Take tram 8 to Veyrier, walk into France, ride the cable car to 1,100 m, then walk the panoramic ridge above Geneva. Wildflowers begin in late April, and the views of Mont Blanc are usually clearer in spring than summer.
activities.seasonal_notes: Snow can linger in patches into early April. Bring layers and sunglasses — wind on the ridge is constant.
activities.booking_tips: Bring passport (cable car is in France). The summit restaurant L'Observatoire serves traditional Savoyard food.

activities.name: Bains des Pâquis Reopening
activities.description: Geneva's beloved pier officially reopens for summer swimming in May. Sunrise dips and breakfast at the kiosk are the local way to mark the start of warm weather.
activities.seasonal_notes: Water still cold (15–17°C) in May. The fondue evenings continue weekly until mid-May.
activities.booking_tips: Towels and swimwear are available to rent. The kiosk serves breakfast from 7am.

activities.name: Salon du Livre Genève
activities.description: Switzerland's biggest French-language book fair, taking over Palexpo with author signings, readings and over 300 publishers.
activities.seasonal_notes: Five-day event. Palexpo is 10 minutes from the centre by tram 5 or train.
activities.booking_tips: Book online for skip-the-queue. The francophone literature stages have simultaneous English translation.

activities.name: Bains des Pâquis Day
activities.description: Spend an entire summer day at the city's signature pier: morning swim, coffee at the kiosk, lunch under umbrellas, afternoon swim, sunset apéro looking at the Jet d'Eau.
activities.seasonal_notes: Pier is open 7am to 10pm. The cafeteria does a fixed-price lunch most days for under CHF 20.
activities.booking_tips: Avoid weekends in late July for crowd reasons — Wednesday mornings are best.

activities.name: Musiques en été Concerts
activities.description: Free open-air concerts in Parc La Grange every evening across July and August — classical, jazz, rock and world music.
activities.seasonal_notes: Bring a blanket and a picnic. Programmes are released early summer. Often features international headliners despite being free.
activities.booking_tips: No tickets required. The lawn fills by 6:30pm for popular nights.

activities.name: Fêtes de Genève Fireworks
activities.description: Geneva's signature summer festival climaxes with a vast fireworks display over the harbour, choreographed to music broadcast through the city — drawing 200,000+ spectators.
activities.seasonal_notes: The festival itself runs about 10 days with food stalls, concerts and rides along the lakefront.
activities.booking_tips: Best view from the Eaux-Vives jetty or the Bains des Pâquis pier. Arrive 2 hours ahead for premium spots.

activities.name: Cycle the Lakefront
activities.description: Easy flat ride from central Geneva north along the lake to Versoix (12 km each way), passing the UN, the Botanical Garden and Genthod's mansions. Excellent stop-off swims along the route.
activities.price_range: Free with hire bike (CHF 12/day from Genève Roule)
activities.seasonal_notes: Geneva runs a free city bike scheme (Genève Roule) at multiple stations.
activities.booking_tips: Pick up bikes at Place du Rhône or Place de Montbrillant. ID required.

activities.name: Stand-Up Paddleboarding Sunset
activities.description: Rent a paddleboard from the Eaux-Vives marina and head out into the harbour as the Jet d'Eau lights up — the most magical 90 minutes in summer Geneva.
activities.seasonal_notes: Lake is glass-flat most evenings in summer. Lifejackets included.
activities.booking_tips: Genève Roule and SUP Geneva both rent boards. Book ahead on weekends.

activities.name: Vendanges (Grape Harvest)
activities.description: Geneva produces more wine than most people realise — over 1,400 hectares around the canton. September brings harvest open days at small estates in Satigny, Russin and Dardagny.
activities.seasonal_notes: Russin's Fête des Vendanges is the canton's signature harvest fair — usually the third weekend of September.
activities.booking_tips: Take the train from Cornavin to Russin (15 min). Walk between cellars; the village shuttle runs harvest weekend.

activities.name: Nuit des Bains
activities.description: Three coordinated evenings each year (one in spring, two in autumn) when 20+ contemporary-art galleries in the Quartier des Bains throw simultaneous openings.
activities.seasonal_notes: Includes MAMCO (free that evening), Galerie Skopia, Mai 36, Galerie Andata Ritorno and many others.
activities.booking_tips: Print the official map from nuitdesbains.ch. Restaurants in the area book up — reserve early.

activities.name: Chasse (Game) Dining
activities.description: From mid-September through November, Geneva's traditional bistros put up special chasse menus — venison, wild boar, hare, partridge — paired with autumn vegetables.
activities.seasonal_notes: The classic order is selle de chevreuil (saddle of venison) with red cabbage, spätzli, chestnut purée and poached pear.
activities.booking_tips: Café du Soleil, Les Armures and Café des Négociants all run chasse menus. Reserve in advance — they fill fast.

activities.name: Jura Crest Hike
activities.description: From Geneva's western edge, the Jura mountains stretch in a long ridge — Crêt de la Neige, La Dôle, the Reculet — with sweeping autumn-colour views.
activities.seasonal_notes: Carry layers — exposed ridge weather changes quickly. Sunrise hikes for the inversion fog spectacle.
activities.booking_tips: La Dôle (1,677 m) is accessible by bus from Saint-Cergue. Train Geneva–Saint-Cergue runs hourly.

activities.name: Course de l'Escalade
activities.description: Switzerland's biggest urban running race, weaving 6 km through the Old Town. Costume race ('Marmite') and kids' races make it a citywide festival.
activities.best_time: First weekend of December (border with autumn)
activities.price_range: CHF 60 to enter; free to watch
activities.seasonal_notes: Over 50,000 runners across two days. Streets in the Old Town close from Friday evening.
activities.booking_tips: Enter at escalade.ch by mid-November. Spectators should head to Place du Bourg-de-Four for finish-line atmosphere.

activities.name: Escalade Festival
activities.description: Geneva's biggest local festival, commemorating the failed 1602 attack by Savoyard forces. Costumed processions, public soup-serving and the smashing of chocolate cauldrons (marmites) filled with marzipan vegetables.
activities.seasonal_notes: Processions weave through the Old Town with torches and drums. The Compagnie de 1602 in period costume runs the show.
activities.booking_tips: Best viewing from Place du Bourg-de-Four. Try the soupe de l'Escalade ladled out for free in the squares.

activities.name: Bains des Pâquis Winter Sauna
activities.description: Winter mornings at the pier are arguably the city's secret ritual: sauna, hammam, and a freezing plunge into the lake while watching the sun rise over the Alps.
activities.seasonal_notes: Sauna is mixed-gender; bring a swimsuit.
activities.booking_tips: Sunrise sessions (7am) book out — reserve at bains-des-paquis.ch up to a week ahead.

activities.name: Day Ski Trips to the French Alps
activities.description: Geneva is the world's best big city for day skiing. Direct buses leave Cornavin and the airport each morning for Les Houches, Flaine, Les Carroz, La Clusaz, Grand-Bornand and Chamonix.
activities.seasonal_notes: Ski-bus runs daily from December through Easter. Bring euros — most French resorts don't accept Swiss francs.
activities.booking_tips: Mountain Drop-offs and Ski Geneva run the most reliable shuttles. Book lift passes online for the queue-skip.

activities.name: Fondue & Raclette Crawl
activities.description: Geneva is fondue capital territory. Build a winter evening around two or three classic spots — Café du Soleil, Les Armures, Edelweiss — and finish with a kirsch.
activities.seasonal_notes: Most fondue restaurants run their full menus only from October to April. Vacherin Mont d'Or is the seasonal star.
activities.booking_tips: All three are bookable online. Edelweiss has live alphorn entertainment most evenings — touristy but unforgettable.

activities.name: Bastions Ice Skating
activities.description: A pop-up outdoor ice rink in the Parc des Bastions runs from late November through January, alongside a small Christmas market.
activities.seasonal_notes: Open daily noon to 10pm. Music and lights make it especially atmospheric at night.
activities.booking_tips: Walk-in only. Park benches around the rink make for good apéro spots.

ranges.notes: Public holiday across Switzerland. Most museums, shops and restaurants closed; lakefront walks and the Bains des Pâquis sauna remain open. Temperatures average around 3°C/37°F.
Quiet post-holiday week. Hotels heavily discounted, lake fog common, daylight only about 9 hours. Excellent for museum-focused trips and Old Town wandering.
Geneva's coldest, foggiest stretch. Lavaux vineyard hikes still accessible; Alps ski day-trips from Geneva to Les Houches or La Clusaz at their best.
Geneva's cross-disciplinary winter arts festival animates clubs, churches and warehouses across the city — its busiest cultural moment of the cold months.
Antigel Festival continues; ski conditions in nearby French Alps remain excellent.
Chocolatiers like Stettler and Du Rhône release Valentine's collections. Restaurants packed mid-week.
Late February brings the first hints of light and crocuses in the Bastions. School winter break (relâches) drives day-trip skiing and family hotel demand.

ranges.event: Geneva International Motor Show (mid-March, biennial — confirm year)
ranges.notes: When the Motor Show runs (biennial years), hotel rates spike and English-speaking crowds peak. In off-years, March is quiet and discounted.
First daffodils, longer evenings, lakefront cafés begin opening terraces. Bains des Pâquis sauna still operating; Easter early-season tourism warming up.

ranges.event: Salon du Livre Genève (typically late March / early April)
ranges.notes: Switzerland's biggest French-language book fair fills Palexpo. Mid-April daffodils and tulips in city parks; Jet d'Eau back to full schedule.
Easter weekend draws regional visitors; chocolatiers in full force. School holiday week boosts family demand and hotel rates.
Public holiday — banks closed, demonstrations on the Plaine de Plainpalais. Some museums and shops on reduced hours.
Best month overall. Bains des Pâquis swimming season opens; Lavaux vineyards in full leaf; Salève accessible without snow; UN tour weather ideal.

ranges.event: Caves Ouvertes Genevoises (last weekend of May)
ranges.notes: Almost every Geneva-canton winemaker opens for tastings — one of the city's most loved weekend events. Free buses connect estates.
Long days (16+ hours), warm lake (~19°C), full restaurant terrace season. Ideal weather for everything except mountain top-of-Europe trips.
Europe's largest inland sailing race — 500+ boats start and finish at the Société Nautique de Genève. Lakefront packed.

ranges.event: Musiques en été (free park concerts, all July)
ranges.notes: Parc La Grange hosts nightly free open-air concerts. Peak swimming season at Bains des Pâquis and Genève-Plage.
Geneva itself hosts free outdoor cinema (CinéTransat on the lakefront) and many smaller summer festivals. Hot, often 30°C+.
Public holiday — flag-themed parades, lakefront fireworks, traditional sausages and red-and-white everywhere.
The lakefront fireworks finale is one of the largest in Europe — 250,000+ spectators around the harbour.
Locals on holiday — many smaller restaurants closed. Excellent weather but a noticeably quieter Geneva than July.
Arguably the best month: warm but not hot (~22°C high), back-to-business pace, vineyards harvesting, lake still swimmable.

ranges.event: Caves Ouvertes Vaudoises and Geneva grape harvest
ranges.notes: Vendanges across the canton; cellar doors open. UN General Assembly week (start in NY but Geneva sees diplomatic spillover).

ranges.event: Nuit des Bains gallery openings (early October)
ranges.notes: Three nights of simultaneous openings in the Quartier des Bains contemporary-art galleries.
Autumn colour peaks in the Bastions and Parc des Eaux-Vives. Chasse (game) season hits Old Town bistros. Cooler, often grey.
Geneva's quietest stretch — short days, often grey, lake fog. Hotels heavily discounted. Best month for museums and a quiet Old Town.

ranges.event: Course de l'Escalade running race (end of November)
ranges.notes: Famous costumed run through the Old Town — over 50,000 participants including children's races. The single biggest sports event in Geneva.
Jardin Anglais market opens; Old Town arcades lit up; Carouge's 'Noël à Carouge' weekend draws crowds.
Geneva's signature holiday: costumed processions through the Old Town, marmite chocolate-pot smashing, soupe de l'Escalade in public squares. The most authentic local moment of the year.
Christmas Day quiet; the days between Christmas and New Year very busy with ski day-trippers and lakeside hotel guests. New Year's Eve fireworks over the lake.

spring: Watch the Bains des Pâquis swimming season reopen (May)
Hike sections of the Lavaux vineyards as the new vintage begins to leaf
Visit Caves Ouvertes Genevoises wine weekends
Take the Salève cable car for first snow-free Alpine views
Cycle the lakefront cycle path from Pâquis to Versoix
summer: Swim and sauna at Bains des Pâquis
Picnic at Parc La Grange during free Musiques en été concerts
Watch Fêtes de Genève fireworks over the harbour
Take the boat to Yvoire or Évian for a half-day in France
Stand-up paddle the harbour at sunset
autumn: Catch Nuit des Bains gallery openings in the Quartier des Bains
Eat chasse (game) menus in Old Town bistros
Hike the Jura crest for autumn colour
Tour the new wine releases at vineyards in Satigny and Russin
Run the Course de l'Escalade or watch from the Old Town
winter: Sunrise sauna at Bains des Pâquis
Ski day-trip to Les Houches, Flaine, La Clusaz or Grand-Bornand
Eat fondue or raclette in the Old Town and Carouge
Smash a chocolate marmite during the Escalade festival
Skate at the Bastions ice rink

scoreDescription.1: Poor: Unfavorable conditions, severe weather, limited operations
scoreDescription.2: Below Average: Quiet, cold or wet, many seasonal closures (museum/restaurant)
scoreDescription.3: Average: Standard conditions, moderate weather, manageable crowds
scoreDescription.4: Good: Pleasant weather, lively atmosphere, many seasonal events
scoreDescription.5: Excellent: Ideal weather, full festival/event calendar, lake activities, long daylight

reasons_to_visit.details: April brings cherry, magnolia, and tulip displays at the Botanical Gardens, Parc des Eaux-Vives, Parc La Grange, and Parc des Bastions — the lakefront feels reborn after winter.
Geneva's flagship book fair at Palexpo (typically late March or April) brings authors, readers, and literary programming from across the Francophonie — a relaxed, well-attended cultural anchor.
CGN's full spring schedule kicks in, including the historic belle-époque paddle steamers running to Nyon, Lausanne, and Yvoire — one of Europe's great affordable scenic day trips.
Average highs of 13–17°C and long evenings make April excellent for walking the Old Town, climbing the cathedral towers, and looping the lake bridges.
Higher resorts (Chamonix, Verbier, Zermatt) ski strong into mid-April, offering uncrowded slopes, soft snow, and lunch on a sunny terrace.
Outside Easter and major Palexpo events, April is one of the year's most pleasant low-cost windows in the city.

reasons_to_reconsider.details: Atlantic systems regularly produce rain bursts and afternoon thunderstorms; an umbrella is essential.
Good Friday and Easter Monday are full public holidays — most shops close and many small restaurants take long weekends.
April is peak season for birch and other tree pollens. Sensitive travellers should bring antihistamines.
Lake Geneva is still in the low teens — swimming remains a brave undertaking. Bains des Pâquis is reopening but not yet summer-busy.
Above 1,500m, trails in the Jura and Salève can still be patchy with snow and mud; consult local trail reports.
UN agencies and watch industry events resume in earnest, lifting weekday hotel demand around Place des Nations and Pâquis.

weather.precipitation: Variable: glorious sunshine alternating with cool showers. Long evenings extend to 20:30 by mid-month.
weather.general_tips: Pack layers, light rain shell, and sunglasses. First T-shirt afternoons on lakefront terraces happen here.

tourism_level.crowds: Building, especially on Easter weekend and during Salon du Livre.
tourism_level.pricing: Soft mid-week; weekend rates climb. Easter weekend spikes if it falls in this window.
tourism_level.overall_atmosphere: Spring optimism. Terraces full at the first hint of sun; runners line the quais.

events_holidays.name: Easter Holidays (when in April)
events_holidays.description: Good Friday and Easter Monday are full public holidays — banks, offices, and most shops closed; museums and lake cruises typically run.
events_holidays.notes: Book restaurants for Easter Sunday brunch early. Many lakeside hotels run Easter packages.

events_holidays.name: Salon du Livre Geneva
events_holidays.description: Major Francophone book fair at Palexpo: authors in conversation, themed pavilions, children's literature programming.
events_holidays.notes: Day tickets affordable. Take Tram 18 or train to Palexpo.

events_holidays.name: Spring Programme at Victoria Hall
events_holidays.description: Orchestre de la Suisse Romande and visiting ensembles fill Victoria Hall with major spring concerts.
events_holidays.notes: Best acoustics in central Geneva. Reduced-price last-minute student tickets sometimes available.

unique_experiences.description: Climb the north tower for one of Geneva's best panoramas — the lake stretching to Lausanne, Mont Blanc to the south, and the Jura to the west.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Tower entry is small fee separate from cathedral entry. Combine with the Site Archéologique beneath the cathedral.

unique_experiences.where: Palexpo (Tram 18 or train one stop from Cornavin)
unique_experiences.description: Browse pavilions of Francophone publishers, sit in on author conversations, and grab a glass of Genevan Chasselas at the wine bar.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Free wardrobes; lunch options on-site fine but pricey.

unique_experiences.description: CGN's restored belle-époque paddle steamers (Savoie, Simplon, Italie) start spring runs in April. Ride 45 minutes along the south shore to the lake-castle town of Nyon for lunch and a Roman museum.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Swiss Travel Pass covers regular routes; first-class supplement is small and includes a calmer top deck.

unique_experiences.where: Conservatoire et Jardin botaniques (Tram 15 to Jardin Botanique)
unique_experiences.description: Free 28-hectare garden with magnolia avenue, alpine rockeries, and protected glasshouses — one of Geneva's most underrated spring assets.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Allow 1.5–2 hours. Lakefront café at the entrance.

weather.precipitation: Generally drier and warmer than early month. Thunderstorms more likely later in the period.
weather.general_tips: Excellent walking and cycling conditions. Sunscreen on clear days; UV climbs quickly.

tourism_level.crowds: Healthy mid-week, busy on weekends. Easter weekend (if late April) very busy.
tourism_level.pricing: Mid-range; lakefront properties book ahead for weekends.
tourism_level.overall_atmosphere: Full spring momentum: cafés full, runners and cyclists out, weekend day trips to Lavaux and Yvoire popular.

events_holidays.name: Geneva Half-Marathon (variable date)
events_holidays.description: Major running event around the lakefront and Old Town, drawing thousands of runners and closing the quais.
events_holidays.notes: Spectacular spectator atmosphere; expect tram and traffic detours.

events_holidays.name: Spring Vintage at Domaine de Châteauvieux
events_holidays.description: Several Geneva-area Michelin-starred restaurants debut spring tasting menus.
events_holidays.notes: Reserve two to four weeks ahead for weekend dinners.

unique_experiences.activity: Cycle the Voie Verte to Annemasse
unique_experiences.where: Eaux-Vives → Annemasse via the Voie Verte greenway
unique_experiences.description: Cross-border bike path along the old railway corridor, with cafés, art murals, and easy connections back to Geneva via Léman Express.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Donkey Republic and Genève Roule rent bikes from Place de Montbrillant.

unique_experiences.activity: Maison Tavel + Old Town Architecture Walk
unique_experiences.description: Geneva's oldest private home houses an excellent city museum, including the famed 1850 Magnin relief map. Combine with a circuit of the Old Town's hidden courtyards.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Closed Mondays. Free permanent collection.

unique_experiences.description: Bigger spring vegetable and flower selection arrives by mid-April. Linger for an apéro on a terrace afterwards.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Tram 12. Wednesday and Saturday morning markets best.

unique_experiences.description: Tour the Science Gateway exhibitions and, if available, book a guided visit deeper into CERN itself.
unique_experiences.practical_tips: Science Gateway free, no reservation for most exhibits. Guided tours book out weeks in advance.

reasons_to_visit.details: The signature mid-August fireworks display over the harbour — one of Europe's largest pyrotechnic shows — anchors days of food markets, concerts, and lakefront installations.
Switzerland's national holiday brings parades, alpenhorn performances, brunch on lake-facing terraces, and fireworks at neighbouring villages around the lake.
With UN, watch industry, and other business travel paused for summer holidays, weekday hotel rates ease meaningfully — one of the best urban value windows of the year.
Lake Geneva peaks at 23–24°C — comfortable extended swimming. All public baths and beaches at full throttle.
Stable late-summer high pressure produces some of the clearest mountain panoramas of the year — perfect for Salève, Mont Blanc, and Jura day trips.
OpenAir Cinema and final Musiques en été nights continue across August evenings.

reasons_to_reconsider.details: Late-summer heat domes are increasingly common. Many Geneva hotels lack robust AC; check before booking.
On fireworks weekend, the lakefront is shoulder-to-shoulder for kilometres. Restaurants near the harbour fully booked; bring patience.
Many small artisan shops, family bakeries, and bistros take 2–3 weeks of holidays in August — especially in Carouge and the Old Town.
Persistent heat triggers powerful afternoon thunderstorms; lake activities and outdoor concerts may cancel.
In recent years, southerly winds have occasionally pushed wildfire smoke into the Léman basin.
August Saturdays are notorious for French motorway gridlock and Geneva airport congestion.

weather.precipitation: Hot and largely dry, with thunderstorm clusters. Lake at 23–24°C.
weather.general_tips: Hydrate; protect against sun and storms. Book Fêtes de Genève dining in advance.
